knowledge.statement: The Factory Method pattern defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. It decouples object creation from the code that uses the object, making it easy to add new types without modifying existing code.

The Abstract Factory pattern provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes. It ensures that products from one factory are compatible with each other.

The Builder pattern separates the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing the same construction process to create different representations. It is useful when an object has many optional parameters or requires multi-step construction.

The Singleton pattern ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point to it. It must be implemented carefully in multithreaded environments to avoid race conditions during initialisation.

The Prototype pattern creates new objects by copying an existing instance. It is useful when object creation is expensive and an existing object can be cloned and then customised rather than created from scratch.

The Adapter pattern converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect. It allows classes with incompatible interfaces to work together, acting as a wrapper that translates calls.

The Bridge pattern decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that both can vary independently. It separates the what (abstraction) from the how (implementation) using composition instead of inheritance.
The Composite pattern composes objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies. Clients treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly through the same interface.
The Decorator pattern attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically. It provides a flexible alternative to subclassing by wrapping objects in decorator objects that add behaviour.
The Facade pattern provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem. It reduces dependencies on the internal components of a subsystem by exposing only the operations clients need.

The Flyweight pattern uses sharing to support a large number of fine-grained objects efficiently. It separates intrinsic state (shared, stored in the flyweight) from extrinsic state (context-dependent, passed by the caller).
The Proxy pattern provides a surrogate for another object to control access to it. Common uses include lazy initialisation, access control, logging, caching, and remote object access.
The Chain of Responsibility pattern passes a request along a chain of handlers. Each handler decides either to process the request or to pass it along the chain, decoupling senders from receivers.
The Command pattern encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterisation, queuing, logging, and undo/redo operations. Commands can be composed into macros and executed asynchronously.
The Iterator pattern provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its underlying representation. It decouples the traversal algorithm from the collection structure.

The Mediator pattern defines an object that encapsulates how a set of objects interact. It promotes loose coupling by preventing objects from referring to each other explicitly, centralising complex communication logic.

The Memento pattern captures and externalises an object's internal state without violating encapsulation, so the object can be restored to that state later. It is the foundation of undo/redo functionality.

The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified automatically. It is the basis for event systems, reactive programming, and the publish-subscribe model.

The State pattern allows an object to alter its behaviour when its internal state changes. The object will appear to change its class because behaviour is delegated to state objects rather than implemented with conditionals.

The Strategy pattern defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. It lets the algorithm vary independently from the clients that use it, replacing complex conditionals with composition.

The Template Method pattern defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, deferring some steps to subclasses. Subclasses can override specific steps without changing the overall algorithm structure.

The Visitor pattern lets you add further operations to objects without modifying them. A visitor object contains the operation logic and visits each element of an object structure, applying the appropriate operation per type.

The Repository pattern provides a collection-like interface for accessing domain objects from a data store. It isolates the domain model from the data access layer, making business logic independent of persistence technology.

The Unit of Work pattern tracks objects affected by a business transaction and coordinates writing out changes and resolving concurrency problems. It batches multiple operations into a single database transaction.

The Data Mapper pattern separates the in-memory objects from the database. A mapper layer transfers data between the two while keeping them independent, unlike Active Record where the object knows how to save itself.

The Active Record pattern wraps a database row in an object that includes both data and behaviour, including database access methods. It is simple for basic CRUD but can violate SRP as domain complexity grows.

The Publish-Subscribe pattern decouples message producers from consumers through a message broker. Producers publish to topics; consumers subscribe to topics. This enables fan-out and allows producers and consumers to evolve independently.

The Event Bus pattern provides a centralised channel for publishing and subscribing to events within a single application. It decouples components but can make tracing event flows and debugging harder if overused.

The Service Locator pattern provides a registry that allows components to look up services by name. It is generally considered an anti-pattern because it hides dependencies, making code harder to test and reason about.

The Dependency Injection pattern passes dependencies to a component rather than having it create or locate them. Constructor injection is the most explicit and testable form; it makes all dependencies visible at instantiation time.

The Null Object pattern uses an object with defined neutral behaviour as a default, instead of using null references. It eliminates null checks throughout the code and avoids NullPointerException errors.

The Object Pool pattern maintains a pool of reusable objects that are expensive to create. Clients borrow objects from the pool and return them after use, reducing allocation overhead for frequently created objects.

The Interpreter pattern defines a grammar for a language and provides an interpreter that uses the grammar to interpret sentences. It is useful for domain-specific languages, expression evaluators, and scripting engines.

The Throttle pattern limits the rate at which a function is called by enforcing a maximum number of invocations per time period. Throttling is typically applied at the API gateway or client side to protect downstream systems.

The Debounce pattern delays executing a function until after a specified period has elapsed since the last time it was invoked. It is useful for search inputs and resize handlers to avoid triggering expensive operations on every keystroke.
The Materialized View pattern pre-computes and stores the results of a query so it can be retrieved quickly without re-running the computation. The view is updated when the underlying data changes.
The Index Table pattern creates secondary indexes over data in stores that do not natively support them. Separate tables are maintained to map lookup keys to the location of the original record.

The Sharding pattern divides a data store into horizontal partitions (shards). Each shard holds a subset of the data, enabling scale-out of storage and throughput. Shard keys must be chosen to distribute load evenly.
The Cache-Aside pattern loads data into the cache on demand. The application checks the cache first; on a miss, it fetches from the database and populates the cache for subsequent reads.
The Write-Through cache pattern writes data to both the cache and the backing store simultaneously. Reads are always served from the cache, ensuring consistency at the cost of write latency.

The Write-Behind (Write-Back) cache pattern writes to the cache immediately and defers the write to the backing store asynchronously. It improves write performance but risks data loss if the cache fails before the write is flushed.
The Competing Consumers pattern allows multiple consumers to process messages from a queue concurrently. It scales message processing horizontally without requiring changes to producers.

The Scheduler Agent Supervisor pattern coordinates distributed work: a Scheduler assigns tasks, Agents execute them, and a Supervisor monitors for failure and re-assigns timed-out tasks. It provides resilience for long-running workflows.

The Webhook pattern allows a server to push real-time notifications to registered URL endpoints when events occur, instead of requiring clients to poll. It is simpler and more efficient than polling for low-frequency events.

The Idempotent Consumer pattern ensures message handlers can safely process the same message multiple times without duplicate effects. It is implemented by recording processed message IDs and skipping duplicates.

The Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) pattern routes messages that cannot be processed successfully to a separate queue for inspection and reprocessing. It prevents poisoned messages from blocking the main processing queue.
The Token Bucket algorithm allows bursts of traffic up to a bucket capacity, then enforces a steady rate by refilling the bucket at a fixed rate. It is commonly used for API rate limiting.

The Sidecar pattern deploys helper functionality in a separate process alongside the main application. It allows adding capabilities (logging, proxying, config) without modifying the main application, promoting reuse across services.

The Valet Key pattern grants clients limited, time-restricted direct access to a specific resource (such as a cloud storage blob) using a token or signed URL. The server generates the token without exposing its own credentials.

The Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) pattern allows multiple transactions to proceed without locking, but checks before commit whether any concurrent modifications have occurred. If a conflict is detected, the transaction is retried.

The Pessimistic Locking pattern acquires a lock on a resource before reading or writing it, preventing other transactions from accessing it until the lock is released. It prevents conflicts but reduces concurrency and risks deadlocks.

The Event-Carried State Transfer pattern embeds enough data in an event so that consumers do not need to query the source service for additional information. This reduces inter-service dependencies and improves resilience.

The Inbox pattern records incoming messages in a database table before processing them. If processing fails, the message is still in the inbox and can be retried, providing at-least-once delivery guarantees.

The Strangler Fig pattern wraps an existing system with a new system that intercepts requests. Functionality is migrated incrementally; the legacy system shrinks as the new system grows, enabling safe replacement without a big-bang rewrite.

The Leader Election pattern designates one instance in a cluster as the coordinator for tasks that should only run on a single node. If the leader fails, remaining instances elect a new leader automatically.

The Snapshot pattern in Event Sourcing periodically saves the current state of an aggregate so that the full event history does not need to be replayed on every load. Events after the snapshot are still replayed to bring state up to date.

The Compensating Transaction pattern undoes the work of a previous transaction when a multi-step process fails partway through. Each step in a Saga has a corresponding compensating transaction to reverse its effect.

The Projection pattern in Event Sourcing builds read-optimised views by replaying events and updating a denormalised read model. Multiple projections can be created from the same event stream to serve different query needs.

The Claim Check pattern replaces a large message payload with a reference (claim check) to data stored externally. The consumer retrieves the payload using the reference, keeping message queues lightweight.

The Health Endpoint Monitoring pattern implements a functional endpoint in an application that external monitoring tools can regularly call to check health. It provides more meaningful status than a simple TCP check.

The Choreography pattern in Saga distributes saga logic across services: each service listens for events and publishes its own events on completion or failure. No central coordinator exists, making the system more loosely coupled.

The CQRS pattern separates the command model (write side) from the query model (read side). This allows each side to be optimised independently and is particularly powerful when combined with Event Sourcing.

The Retry-with-Jitter pattern adds random variation to retry intervals. Without jitter, all failed requests from multiple clients retry simultaneously, creating thundering herd problems on the recovering service.
